ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301638 (cocoa beverage) is the closest defensible match; no exact FoodOn term exists for unsweetened/no-sugar chocolate drink variant.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX (cocoa-based beverage) used as closest defensible match for unsweetened chocolate drink; no exact code for no-sugar variant confirmed in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for unsweetened chocolate drink beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 ml (one cup), equivalent to 200 g, which is a typical single-serve portion for a hot or cold chocolate drink.

nutrient_source.title: Chocolate Drink (No Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chocolate Drink (No Sugar), CR ID D04020003. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Cocoa-based beverage, unsweetened — GI analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for low-GI cocoa/chocolate beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry found for 'Chocolate Drink (No Sugar)' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is estimated from the closest analogous entry for unsweetened cocoa-based beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 35–45 for low-sugar cocoa drinks. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 12.62) / 100 ≈ 5.0 for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (40 mg/100 g) and absence of added sugar
hypertension.guidance: With only 40 mg sodium per 100 g and no added sugar, this unsweetened chocolate drink is suitable for individuals managing hypertension; however, patients should verify that any creamer or milk added during preparation does not significantly increase sodium or saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low sugar (1.94 g/100 g), low GI (~40), and moderate dietary fibre (0.97 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low sugar content, low glycemic index (~40), and absence of added sugar make this unsweetened chocolate drink acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control is still advised, and patients should confirm that no high-GI sweeteners are present in the specific product formulation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low energy density (46 kcal/100 g), low sodium, and cocoa flavonoids with potential cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low caloric density, minimal sodium, and cocoa-derived flavonoids (polyphenols) in this unsweetened chocolate drink may offer modest cardioprotective benefits; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid adding full-fat creamers or sugar, which would negate these benefits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cocoa flavonoids with anti-inflammatory properties; no added sugar
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cocoa powder contains flavonoids with documented anti-inflammatory properties, and the absence of added sugar avoids pro-inflammatory dietary patterns; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should ensure the product does not contain palm-based creamers, which may be pro-inflammatory.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate Drink (No Sugar) is an unsweetened beverage prepared by dissolving pure cocoa powder in water or milk, without the addition of any sugar or caloric sweeteners. It is commonly consumed as a hot or cold drink and is widely available in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a lower-calorie alternative to regular sweetened chocolate drinks. The drink has a mild, slightly bitter cocoa flavour with a thin to medium body depending on the ratio of cocoa to liquid and whether milk or water is used as the base. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 46 kcal, 1.45 g protein, 1.45 g fat, 6.31 g carbohydrate, 1.94 g sugar, 0.97 g dietary fibre, and 40 mg sodium, as reported in the Singapore HPB Foodo database (CR ID D04020003). The low sugar content (1.94 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~40) make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. Cocoa powder is a natural source of flavonoids (polyphenols), which have been associated with anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective effects in peer-reviewed literature. This no-sugar variant is particularly relevant for health-conscious consumers and clinical dietary management contexts where sugar restriction is required. It should be distinguished from regular sweetened chocolate drinks, which contain significantly higher sugar and caloric content.
common_user_queries: Is chocolate drink without sugar good for diabetics?
Is no-sugar chocolate drink suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (CR ID D04020003) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for unsweetened cocoa-based beverages, which slightly reduces overall confidence for the GI component, but the core nutrient data confidence remains high.
notes: 1. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source (CR ID D04020003). These are confirmed zero values per the HPB Foodo database entry; they likely reflect the base cocoa-powder-and-water formulation without milk fortification. If prepared with milk, calcium and iron values would differ. 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields beyond calcium and iron are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin, and extended mineral data for this specific no-sugar chocolate drink are not available in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this entry. 4. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for unsweetened cocoa-based beverages; no exact GI entry for this specific product was identified. 5. The food is classified as a generic beverage record (record_type: generic) from HPB Singapore. Vegan flag is set to false because the description references milk as a possible preparation base; if prepared with water only, the drink may be vegan. 6. Multilingual local names are not applicable for this generic international beverage category; no Singaporean-dish multilingual requirement applies.
